text: The thoracoscopic view reveals the vertebra adjacent to the esophagus, highlighting the anatomical structures involved in the esophagectomy procedure.
The video shows the arch of the azygos vein, a prominent vascular structure adjacent to the esophagus during the thoracoscopic esophagectomy.
The trachea is visible beneath the peritoneal layer during the thoracoscopic esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the subclavian vessel located at the superior aspect of the surgical field.
The thoracoscopic view reveals the intercostal spaces and rib indentations, with the diaphragm visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins by making a small incision on the peritoneum, exposing underlying structures for further dissection.
The surgeon makes a small incision on the dorsal aspect using a monopolar hook, allowing carbon dioxide to enter the plane and expand the space, revealing the area for further incision.
The surgeon identifies a fatty structure located superior to the tip of the monopolar hook, indicating the area where the thoracic duct is situated.
Carbon dioxide insufflation visibly enters the surgical planes, expanding and revealing the avascular space for dissection.
In the dissection field, small vessels are visible on the posterior aspect, identified as direct branches from the aorta. A vessel is seen to the right, near the functioning tip of the instrument.
The surgeon uses a simple hook instrument to take down a very small vessel in the dissection field. For slightly larger vessels, a bipolar or vessel sealer would be preferred.

The surgeon continues the dissection by incising the peritoneum on the dorsal aspect, applying traction to allow carbon dioxide to enter and expand the dissection plane. The fixed peritoneum provides counter traction, aiding in the visualization of the area for further dissection.
The surgeon exposes a larger vessel, identified as the aorta, within the dissection field, highlighting its size and location relative to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to dissect beyond a direct branch of the vessel originating from the aorta. Due to the vessel's larger size, a Ligasure vessel sealer is employed to secure and seal the vessel.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to effectively seal a vessel, preventing retractile bleeding. After the vessel is taken down, the carbon dioxide insufflation visibly expands the dissection space, guiding the direction for further dissection.
The surgeon uses energy devices to ensure no larger vessels remain, then focuses on mobilizing and releasing the peritoneum towards the hiatus.
The surgeon halts the dissection just before completely separating the hiatus from the thoracic part to prevent carbon dioxide from escaping into the abdominal cavity.
The patient is positioned prone, with working and camera ports established in the fifth, sixth, and seventh intercostal spaces for thoracic access during the esophagectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a 5mm telescope through one of the three 5mm ports to visualize the surgical field. The inferior pulmonary ligament is being dissected using a monopolar hook cautery, demonstrating that sophisticated energy devices are not necessary for this part of the procedure.

The surgeon continues to dissect and release the inferior pulmonary ligament, with a small portion of the lung parenchyma becoming visible in the surgical field. Care is taken to reorient the dissection away from the lung to prevent injury.

The surgeon adjusts traction and counter-traction near the lung parenchyma to clearly identify the dissection area. The small peritoneum between structures is dissected to release the inferior pulmonary ligament, facilitating esophagus mobilization.

The surgeon uses traction with the left hand to open up avascular planes for dissection, minimizing oozing. The inferior pulmonary ligament is fully released, revealing the pulmonary vein posterior to the dissection area.

The surgeon identifies the pulmonary vein, visible with a bluish hue and transmitted pulsations from cardiac activity. An incision is made on the peritoneum on the ventral side, with careful attention to the proximity of the pulmonary vein.

The surgeon identifies the thin white structure below the hook as the vagus nerve and carefully dissects above it, making an incision on the peritoneum without penetrating deeper into the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum to further mobilize the esophagus, revealing the trachea as a boitis structure posterior to the dissection area.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the trachea, which appears collapsed and gradually expands, ensuring no transmitted injury occurs from the use of cautery in this sensitive area.

The surgeon makes small incisions to release the peritoneum, ensuring the dissection remains superficial without penetrating deeper layers. The surgical field appears avascular, with precise sharp dissection using energy devices, maintaining clear tissue planes.
The surgeon identifies the trachea, visible as a white structure inferior to the current working area with the hook. The trachea appears collapsed, showing its membranous portion.

The surgeon approaches the arch of the azygos vein, dividing the peritoneum and surrounding tissues up to the arch. The trachea is visible, opening up as the surgeon holds the arch of the azygos and performs dissection behind it to release peri-esophageal tissues, aiming for an en bloc dissection.

The surgeon performs dissection around the esophagus below the arch of the azygos, ensuring complete mobilization of the esophagus while maintaining oncological principles. The dissection is aimed at achieving accurate staging without compromising the surrounding structures.

The surgeon continues dissection beyond the esophagus, guided by carbon dioxide insufflation. The transmitted pulsations of the heart become visible, and the pericardium starts to appear in the surgical field, indicating progress beyond the encircled portion of the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a hook to sharply dissect loose areolar tissues, revealing the pericardium from the opposite side. The dissection has encircled the esophagus completely, with visible tissue planes opening up.

The surgeon performs ventral dissection near the pericardium, visible with transmitted cardiac pulsations causing movement of the esophagus. The assisting hand provides traction while short bursts of cautery are used to meticulously open tissue planes.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the left lung through a thin, translucent pleura, which appears as a blackish discoloration in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully applies counter traction to avoid opening the pleura on the opposite side during dissection, ensuring the pleura remains intact while manipulating the esophageal area.

The surgeon maintains the integrity of the pleura while applying traction on the esophagus using an atraumatic instrument. The dissection continues with the use of a monopolar hook to easily take down small branches running through the area, with no major blood vessels visible.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform dissection below the visible edge of the pleura, releasing surrounding tissues to facilitate the mobilization of the esophagus. The hook's low profile and small exposed tip allow precise cautery application while maintaining insulation to prevent unintended spread.

The surgeon identifies a dissection plane and continues dissecting towards the cranial end, maintaining proximity to the clear edge of the pleura. Care is taken to avoid opening the pleura while releasing peri-esophageal tissues for effective mobilization.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to open avascular planes near the pleura of the left lung, revealing the thin membrane covering the lung. The peri-esophageal tissues are identified, and the vagus nerve is visible on the left side, with the hook instrument pointing towards the pleura.

The edge of the pleura is clearly defined, and the surgeon carefully avoids opening it while performing further dissection towards the cranial aspect. The peritoneum is incised and released to facilitate circular mobilization of the esophagus from the hiatus to the thoracic inlet. The course of the vagus nerve is also visible during the dissection.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction on the esophagus using an atraumatic instrument, revealing the edge of the esophagus and facilitating further dissection. The visible nerve structure is identified, and carbon dioxide insufflation aids in opening up the dissection planes.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection between the aorta and the left bronchus, visible beyond the dissection area. Fatty tissue and small branches from the aorta are carefully dissected using short bursts of cautery. Blunt traction is applied to push off tissues, maintaining dissection in avascular planes. The aorta is clearly visible above the surgical instrument.
The surgeon navigates a narrow space with careful control of the dissection instrument to avoid injury to vascular structures. The left bronchus is visible beyond the current area of dissection.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to release fibrous attachments around the esophagus, facilitating further dissection towards the cranial portion. The carbon dioxide insufflation aids in opening up the tissue planes, allowing for a comprehensive dissection.

The surgeon applies traction to open up the surgical planes, revealing the pericardium. The lymphovascular tissues surrounding the esophagus are being carefully dissected and removed along with the specimen.

The surgeon applies traction to pull the esophagus towards the working port, freeing the pericardium. The edge of the pleura is clearly visible, and care is taken to avoid injury. The pleura is teased away, and surrounding tissues are dissected using a monopolar hook.
The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to clearly visualize the edge of the pleura and continues dissection in the plane, revealing the bar pericardium.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the fatty tissue located between the left pleura and the esophagus, moving it towards the specimen for removal. The traction on the lymphovascular tissues is adjusted to facilitate further dissection, progressing bit by bit.

The surgeon carefully dissects fatty tissue surrounding the esophagus, working directly on the pericardium to ensure hemostatic control and avoid excessive bleeding. The meticulous resection aims to prevent repeated steps and ensure efficient progress.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to reveal the bare pericardium, free of surrounding tissues. A blackish structure, identified as the infracranial group of lymph nodes, becomes visible, typically appearing black in patients with a history of smoking.

The surgeon carefully dissects the soft, reactive lymph nodes, avoiding direct traction to prevent bleeding. Surrounding tissues are used to hold the nodes, while small fibers are meticulously taken down bit by bit during the dissection.

The surgeon holds the esophagus and performs dissection on its ventral aspect to improve visibility of structures from the opposite side. The pulmonary vein is identified, and dissection is carried out between the esophagus and the pulmonary vein.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook in short, intermittent bursts to release fibrous attachments, gradually advancing the dissection. The surgical field is focused on the pulmonary vein, requiring careful energy application and meticulous dissection to avoid damage.

Small vascular branches are visible in the surgical field. The surgeon carefully manipulates the surrounding tissues to avoid blunt injury, which could cause significant oozing and obscure the dissection planes.

The surgeon removes the last of the fatty tissue, revealing the pericardium clearly on the right side. The esophagus is being mobilized, with small adhesions on the ventral aspect being released. As the esophagus is lifted, the translucency of the dissected area on the opposite side becomes visible. Small fibrous attachments are sharply dissected using a monopolar hook.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the caudal end, removing fatty tissue over the pericardium along with the esophageal specimen.

The surgeon identifies the limits of the dissection and continues to work beneath the esophagus, retracting it dorsally to access the ventral portion. The area contains flimsy tissues, which are being dissected using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques with a monopolar hook.

The surgeon shifts focus to the cranial end of the esophagus, dissecting the area between the bronchus. The intracranial lymph node group is carefully dissected, with lymph nodes being removed for sampling. The vagus nerve is clearly visible during this process.

The surgeon performs dissection between the bronchus and the pericardium on the right side, removing fibrous attachments from the fatty tissue along with the specimen. The pericardium on the opposite side is clearly visible.

The surgeon identifies small vessels surrounding the esophagus, with no major vessels or important structures in the immediate area. The membranous portion of the trachea becomes visible, and the surgeon carefully releases surrounding tissues to delineate the extent of the trachea, ensuring its protection during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies lymph nodes and uses a monopolar hook to release fibrous attachments between the lymph nodes and the trachea on the left side. The white structure visible in the field is the trachea, indicating close proximity during dissection.

The laparoscopic view shows the membranous trachea and the left bronchus, with the surgeon carefully dissecting lymph nodes adjacent to these structures. The magnification makes the area appear farther away, but the dissection is occurring very close to the trachea, requiring caution to avoid thermal injuries. The surgeon progresses in removing lymphovascular structures.
The surgeon releases lymphovascular structures, gradually incorporating the entire lymph nodal block into the specimen. The dissection progresses with visible separation of tissue layers.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to better visualize the bronchus, releasing small tissues around it. The lymph node and surrounding fatty tissue are dissected away, revealing the entire bronchus clearly.

The surgeon reaches the bifurcation point where the trachea divides into the left and right bronchus, clearly visible in the surgical field. The dissection is focused at the junction of the bronchial division.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect near the left bronchus, revealing fleshy tissues and small vessels in the area.
The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining fibrotic attachments holding the lymph node in place near the left bronchus, gradually releasing the node from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects fibrous tissue near the membranous portion of the trachea using monopolar energy, ensuring a safe distance from the bronchus. Traction is applied to separate the fibers, revealing small vessels in the area.

The surgeon meticulously coagulates small vessels to achieve hemostasis while dissecting the subcarinal group of lymph nodes. Most lymph nodes have been removed, with only a small portion remaining at the bifurcation level.

The surgeon applies traction to the esophagus, using the fixed membranous portion of the trachea for counter traction, facilitating safe dissection. The esophagus is largely mobilized, with a small portion of dissection remaining near the right bronchus. The esophagus is lifted carefully without direct handling.

The surgeon manipulates periesophageal tissues to identify the correct dissection plane, carefully dissecting lymphovascular tissue from the anterior aspect between the esophagus and pericardium. The pericardium is exposed clearly, indicating comprehensive oncological clearance. The vagus nerve becomes visible during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects below the bifurcation level, avoiding the vagus nerve to prevent injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The dissection plane is identified, revealing lymph nodes with a typical black coloration and fleshy appearance.

The surgeon avoids directly holding the friable lymph nodes to prevent oozing, instead using perilymph nodal and peristructural tissues, such as those around the lung and esophagus, for traction and counter-traction. This technique reveals clear planes for dissection, allowing for the excision of lymphovascular structures along with the specimen.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to divide fibrotic strands and small vessels, improving the mobility of lymph nodes and surrounding structures. The lung and bronchus are gently pushed aside to increase the safety margin, facilitating lymph node clearance in the infracarinal portion near the right bronchus.

The pulmonary vein is clearly visible, showing distinct pulsations. The pericardium is located just lateral to the vein from the current viewing angle. Small branches of vessels are observed running in the area, which are being meticulously coagulated and divided to ensure a secure procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects small lymph nodes near the bifurcation, ensuring they are included with the specimen. The dissection is performed meticulously, similar to the approach on the left side, with attention to detail to avoid injury to surrounding structures.

The membranous portion of the bronchus is visibly bulging as it joins the tracheal portion. The surgeon uses cautery in short bursts, minimizing fumes and reducing charge buildup at the instrument tip to prevent lateral spread and potential injury to adjacent organs.

The surgeon identifies the dissection plane between the vagus nerve and peri-esophageal tissue to mobilize the esophagus up to the thoracic inlet. The bronchus bifurcation from the trachea is visible, and the surgeon works carefully above the bronchus, mindful of the proximity to the membranous portion of the trachea to avoid injury while using cautery.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to reveal the avascular spaces, applying traction and counter-traction to expose the bifurcation area clearly. The bifurcation is identified, showing the anatomical separation of structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects lymph nodes near the bifurcation area, identifying fibrotic tissues and small vessels. A vessel sealing device, the ligature, is used to minimize thermal spread, particularly near the membranous portions of the bronchus and trachea. Suction is applied to clear the area and precisely delineate the bronchus, preventing accidental injury.

The surgeon leaves a small cup of normal tissue near the bronchus to avoid thermal injury during dissection. Suction is used to clear oozing, ensuring the bronchus is well-identified. Lymphovascular structures are included with the specimen before coagulating and dividing small branches and fibrotic tissues in the area.

The surgeon dissects on the left side of the bronchus, just below the bifurcation area, to identify the border of the bronchus. The lymphovascular tissue attachments are carefully released to ensure safe dissection without damaging the bronchus.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the esophagus, pushing it laterally to avoid injury to the left bronchus. Blood is suctioned from the area, revealing the bifurcation level, which is cleared of lymphovascular structures. Remaining tissue tethered to the left bronchus is dissected away, ensuring no inadvertent injury to vital structures.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to release the last few attachments at the bifurcation area, completing the lymph nodal clearance. The infracranial portion becomes free, revealing the pericardium and left bronchus clearly. The end block tissue, including the esophagus, is visible after the dissection.

The surgeon suctions the area to check for bleeding or oozing, confirming that there is no major bleeding present. The lymphovascular tissues have been completely cleared. The dissection progresses to the supra-esophageal portion, mobilizing the esophagus near the thoracic inlet for complete mediastinal mobilization.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum in the supra-azygus portion using thoracoscopy, creating space for ligation of the azygus vein. The arch of the azygus is clearly visible during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the esophagus while avoiding injury to the azygos vein. The dissection reveals the right bronchial artery in close proximity, and the surgeon works above the trachea, creating a clear window for ligating the azygos vein.

The surgeon performs ligation along the direction of vessel flow from dorsal to ventral, starting with the dorsal aspect. A square knot is tied below the vessel's ascending and descending portions, leaving a small stump for secure ligature. The vein visibly collapses following the ligation.

The surgeon uses a suture passer to manipulate the vein, pulling it upward to elongate the stump for ligation. After securing the ligation, scissors are used to divide the vein, leaving a small cuff of approximately five millimeters on either side to ensure the knot remains secure during manipulation.

The surgeon uses an endo loop for double ligation of the vein, ensuring security. After dividing the vein, the superior portion opens up, revealing the dissection planes. The surgeon holds the edge of the peritoneum, applying traction while the fixed portion provides counter traction. Carbon dioxide insufflation enhances the visibility of the planes, creating a three-dimensional view for safe surgical repair.

The surgeon observes the bulging and pulsation of the subclavian vessels, with the superior vena cava visible laterally. The trachea is exposed, and lymphovascular tissues and peritoneum are held and incised to complete the dissection up to the thoracic inlet using thoracoscopy.

The surgeon has dissected and incised the peritoneum up to the thoracic inlet. The lung is visibly collapsed due to pneumothorax. The surgical field shows the superior aspect of the trachea and the arch of the azygos region, which is being taken down.

The surgeon lifts the esophagus, carefully dissecting and removing small vessels and fibrotic strands between the trachea and esophagus. The right bronchial artery is visible near the azygos level, and efforts are made to preserve it to maintain lung function.

The surgeon uses the tip of a hook to gently push aside the right bronchial artery, which is clearly visible as the dissection progresses. Lymphovascular tissues are being removed to enhance visibility of the artery's course. The telescope orientation is adjusted to maintain a clear view of the bronchial artery for preservation.

The surgeon identifies and dissects fibrotic strands near the trachea, with visible pulsation of the right bronchial artery. The hook is used carefully to avoid injury to nearby structures, including the aorta and trachea.

The surgeon continues dissection directly above the trachea, mobilizing small fibrotic strands to free the esophagus in the supra-azygos region. The vagus nerve is clearly visible during this process.

The surgeon cauterizes small branches and fibrotic strands holding the tissues between the trachea and esophagus, enhancing mobility. The right bronchial artery is visible, with its pulsation clearly observed.

The surgeon holds the tissue around the bronchial artery, revealing its entire course. The artery's path changes when traction is released, highlighting the importance of distinguishing it from other structures to avoid injury.

The surgeon inspects the esophagus for any remaining fibrous tissue attachments, revealing minimal tissue holding on. Dissection is performed to expose the bronchial artery clearly. The membranous portion of the trachea appears collapsed and begins to inflate with positive pressure from the ventilator.

The surgeon carefully dissects small fibrous strands between the esophagus and trachea, ensuring complete mobilization of the esophagus from the thoracic portion, preparing for further dissection in the neck area.

The surgeon dissects lymphovascular tissues located on top of the trachea, preparing to remove them along with the esophagus. Small strands of lymphovascular tissue are visible and are being carefully taken down.

The surgeon points to the subclavian artery, clearly visible in the surgical field, with the left recurrent laryngeal nerve hooking over it. Below the subclavian artery, the superior vena cava is identified by its bluish hue, with the nerve running clearly on top. This marks the completion of the thoracoscopic dissection phase.
An ICD tube is placed to reduce pneumothorax, visible in the post-operative field.
